Tafseer:
1. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

The Hour has come near, and the moon has split. (1)

Some Aspects of the Resurrection and the Decisive Hour Drawing Near

(A messenger holds the position of God's court for his nation). 'Al-Sa'ah' (the Hour) refers to the Day of decision and punishment of which the Quraysh, though warned in all the Surahs of this group, were specifically shaken in the preceding Surah: 'The approaching Day has approached' (57)—meaning: 'this approaching Day has drawn near. Do not consider it far; wake up, rise, and follow the only way described to you to protect yourselves from its terrors.' We have been clarifying in various places that the messengers of Allah warned their nations of two punishments: one which necessarily comes upon the nation in this world if they disregard the messenger's warning and persist on denial; and the second which they will face in the Hereafter. The difference between these two is only of beginning and completion, introduction and finality. The nation seized in the punishment for denying the messenger is in reality seized for the punishment of the Hereafter; for this reason, the word 'Al-Sa'ah' (the Hour) sometimes encompasses both of these punishments. From this perspective, it emerges that for every nation to which a messenger came, its Day of decision arrived. Therefore, 'The Hour has come near' (Iqtarabatis-Sa'ah) is no exaggeration, but the precise description of reality.

A Sign of the Manifestation of the Punishment

'and the moon has split' (wan-shaqqal-qamar)—this is described as a sign of the Hour of punishment drawing near. We have referred in various places to the Sunnah of Allah: although His signs of power and wisdom are present in every corner of the heavens and the earth, and new signs also continuously appear, yet during the period of the messengers' advent, Allah Almighty specifically manifests such signs as reveal the truthfulness of the messenger's warning and his claim to messengership. In the Qur'an, this Sunnah of Allah has been mentioned in various places; we present a verse as an example: 'We will show them Our signs in the horizons and within themselves' (Fussilat: 53). The objective of these signs, as we have pointed out, is to support the messenger's warning. The realities which the messenger proclaims with his tongue are supported by those signs of his power appearing in various forms in the universe so that the proof of Allah is fully established against the people. A similar sign appeared in support of the Prophet's (pbuh) warning in the form of the moon splitting—so that the deniers of the punishment and the Resurrection understood that the warnings of the Qur'an (that the earth will be shaken on that Day, the mountains will be crushed to fly in the atmosphere like dust, the oceans will boil, and the sun will darken) are not said to overawe them, but are realities which are bound to happen, and are not far from possibility, as their proofs appear in some form in this world as well.

For such signs, it is not necessary that the messenger presented them as his miracle; rather, their manifestation can occur without any prior proclamation or challenge. It is also not necessary that the disbelievers specifically demanded the sign which appeared; rather, they can be manifested without any demand from them, purely to answer their doubts. The primary reason why the disbelievers considered the Resurrection far from reason was: how was it possible that this entire universe would be completely disrupted one day? From the question regarding the mountains quoted in the Qur'an, it is learned that they considered these things entirely permanent, unshakeable, and eternal. Allah Almighty, by showing them the sign of the moon splitting, informed them that none of the things of this universe, no matter how great, is independent, eternal, or unshakeable; but everything is subject to the command of Allah. He will disrupt all of them whenever He pleases.

Answering a Question

As for the question: did any such event occur during the Prophet's (pbuh) time? In my view, from the words of the Qur'an, it emerges that it did occur, and this is supported by the Hadiths. Regarding the actual form of the event, the Hadiths do differ, but regarding the occurrence of the event itself, no difference is narrated. Some have said that this is a report of the event to occur on the Day of Judgment, expressed in the past tense to show its certainty. In their view, the meaning is: 'the Hour of Resurrection has drawn near and the moon will split.' Although this view has been narrated from some predecessors and enjoys acceptance among a group in this modern era, the context of the discourse is contrary to it. There is no doubt that the events of the Day of Judgment are expressed in the past tense in the Qur'an, but if this meaning is taken here, the discourse becomes disconnected from the subsequent verse. Below, it is stated that even if they see any sign, they will turn away from it and say: 'This is nothing new, it is magic which has been coming from before.' Reflect: if the moon splitting had related to the Day of Judgment, was there any room to say this? On the Day of Judgment, even the bitterest denier will not be able to call anything magic, but all will admit that what the messengers warned of was true—as is stated below: 'The disbelievers will say, "This is a difficult Day."' (8).

"And if they see a sign, they turn away and say, 'Continuous magic.'" (2)

Consolation for the Prophet (pbuh)

This is consolation for the Prophet (pbuh) and reproach to the deniers—stating that the cause of their denial is not that they have not been shown any sign. Signs are present at every step, and they are continuously being shown new signs, but these stubborn people do not want to believe in reward and punishment; because of this, they take no lesson from even the greatest sign. Even if the Prophet (pbuh) showed them a sign greater than the splitting of the moon, they would declare it the miracle of magic and ignore it.

The Meaning of 'Mustamirr' (Continuous)

With 'magic' (sihr), the attribute 'continuous' (mustamirr) shows the reality: they will not only call this sign magic, but to deceive people, will also say: 'Even as magic, there is no uniqueness in it to be given any importance; rather, this is that very same magic which previous magicians showed and which has been continuously transmitted.' The meaning is: because of this sign, let alone accepting him as a warner from God, there is no reason to consider him even an extraordinary magician. Some have interpreted 'mustamirr' as fleeting/transient, but I have no knowledge of its usage in this sense.

"And they denied and followed their desires. And every matter is settled." (3)

The Wisdom in Granting Respite to the Disbelievers

This describes their denial, the cause of their denial, and their end—stating that they (the Quraysh) denied the warner of God and his warnings and followed their desires, because of which they have deserved the punishment which previous nations faced; but with Allah, every work occurs in accordance with His specified program. When their appointed term is complete, they will be seized. That is: the respite they are receiving does not mean that the Prophet's (pbuh) warning is an empty threat, but rather the time He has appointed for their destruction has not arrived yet, though it has drawn near.

The Cause of Denial

After 'And they denied' (Wa-kadh-dhaboo), the words 'and followed their desires' (wattabi'oo ahwa'ahum) point to the cause of their denial—showing that they denied the messenger's warnings because they are slaves of their desires. If they accepted reward and punishment, they would have to sacrifice these desires, which they do not have the courage to do; because of this, they are making various excuses. This has passed under verse 29 of Surah An-Najm.

"And there has already come to them of information that in which is deterrence -" (4)

The Wise takes Warning from the Fate of Others

This explains the preceding verse—stating: if they have not been punished yet, it does not mean any change has occurred in the Sunnah of Allah. If they reflect on the stories of previous nations, they will find sufficient lessons within them. From these stories, they will know: whichever nation denied the messenger's warnings was ultimately seized in His punishment. Due to the delay in punishment, those nations also fell into the same misconception: that the messenger's warning was an empty threat; but it turned true. The meaning is: it is in their interest to take warning from history, instead of waiting for everything to pass over their heads to believe, as believing at that time was of no use to previous nations and will be of no use to them.

The stories of these nations have passed briefly and in detail in the previous Surahs, and in this Surah also, they are coming from verse 9. There you will see that after every story, the verse: 'So how was My punishment and My warning?' (Fakayfa kana 'adhabee wa-nudhur) has occurred repeatedly, so that it is made clear to the addressees: the truth of the messenger's warning is thus proven, and those who deny are thus seized in the punishment.

"Profound wisdom - but the warners do not avail [them]." (5)

That is, within these stories lies a highly heart-touching wisdom, but see, what benefit are these warnings providing! 'Ma' (not/what) can be negative or interrogative; but there is greater force and contextual suitability in the interrogative meaning.

"So leave them, [O Muhammad]. The Day the Caller calls to a terrible thing," (6)

Consolatory Instruction to the Prophet (pbuh)

This is a consolatory instruction to the Prophet (pbuh)—stating: opening the eyes of such blind people and showing them the path is beyond your power. Turn away from them and await that Day when Israfeel will blow the Horn and they will be called to the punishment of the Resurrection. 'Tawalla' (turn away) here encompasses the meaning of awaiting. This same theme is expressed in another place as follows: 'And listen on the Day when the Caller calls from a near place' (Qaf: 41). By 'a terrible thing' (shai'in nukur), the terror of the Resurrection is pointed to. To express the intensity of its terror, the style of implication is adopted—meaning: today they do not care about the invitation to prepare for that Day, but soon that time is coming when a caller will call to that highly terrifying thing, and all will run toward it with obedience and fear.

Depicting the Exit from the Graves after the Blowing of the Horn

That is: today, when the messenger of Allah invites them to prepare for the terror of that Day to save themselves, they turn away with arrogance; but when the caller of the Resurrection calls, they will exit from their graves like a swarm of locusts, and their state will be such that their heads will be held down in disgrace, and they will be running toward the caller with haste.

"Their eyes humbled, they will emerge from the graves as if they were locusts spreading," (7)

'Khushsha'an' (humbled) and 'muhtee'een' (hastening) are both in the state (hal). 'as if they were locusts spreading' (ka-annahum jaradunm-muntashir) is the comparison of their exit from the graves after the blowing of the Horn. In the rainy season, you must have seen insects emerging from the ground in the evening; it appears as if a flood of insects has emerged from the earth. The same is the case with the emerging of locusts, and the same will be the form of people's exit from their graves. Allah Almighty has shown His glories in this world specifically so that man is reminded of the Resurrection from every corner of the heavens and the earth. 'The disbelievers will say, "This is a difficult Day"' (8)—meaning today, when the messenger of Allah warns them of the terror of that Day, they consider everything improbable and mock it, but when that Day comes, they will cry out with great regret: 'Indeed, that difficult Day of which the messengers warned has arrived!'

2. Subsequent Theme: Verses 9–17

Above in verse 4: 'And there has already come to them of information that in which is deterrence,' the Quraysh were warned: why are they demanding the punishment, why do they not take warning from the history of previous nations! To explain this, the history of the messengers and their nations is described—showing: like you, those nations also mocked their messengers' warnings; ultimately, they fell under that punishment of which Allah's messenger warned; so it is better that you do not follow those nations to invite your own ruin, but receive reminding from the Book He sent down out of His favor for your teaching. This Book is equipped with all accessories for the purpose of teaching. Recite the verses.

3. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

"The people of Noah denied before them, and they denied Our servant and said, 'A madman,' and he was repelled." (9)

Pointing to the Fate of the People of Nuh

We pointed out above that the brief reference to the history of nations in verse 4 is explained here. First, the story of Hazrat Nuh's (peace be upon him) nation is taken up, because the sequence here is historical. It states: before them (the Quraysh), the people of Nuh also denied the messenger of Allah. This consoled the Prophet (pbuh)—showing that you are not the first to face denial from your nation. The history of the deniers is very old; before them, the people of Nuh denied their messenger, and other nations whose details are coming also denied their messengers. The meaning is: this is an old and famous story of history; what happened to previous messengers will happen to you, and the end faced by previous nations will be faced by your nation if they follow their way.

After 'And they denied' (fa-kadh-dhaboo), 'We' (Our) points to the details—showing that they denied Our servant, declared him crazy, and he was repelled. The special love of Allah Almighty for Hazrat Nuh (peace be upon him) in 'Our servant' (abdana) is obvious. When the messengers warned the wealthy of the world of the punishment of this world and the Hereafter, and warned with such intensity as to dedicate their day and night, this was highly painful to them. they did not understand how and from where the punishment would come upon them! If they deserved punishment, why did they receive wealth and glory? Their power and wealth was proof to them that they were close to God, and if the Hereafter occurred, they would be as close there as they were here. Under this misconception, they became so enraged by the messenger's continuous warnings that they declared him crazy—claiming: 'this person has got a mania of the punishment. He sees it coming from every corner.' By saying this, they expressed the anger of their hearts and tried to convince their common people that the messenger's warning was only a mania. 'and he was repelled' (waz-dujir) points to those threats of stoning which were given to Hazrat Nuh (peace be upon him). For example, in Surah Ash-Shu'ara': 'They said, "If you do not desist, O Noah, you will surely be of those who are stoned."' (116).

"So he called upon his Lord, 'Indeed, I am defeated, so defend [me].'" (10)

When the nation's enmity reached the extent that they threatened to stone Hazrat Nuh (peace be upon him), he supplicated to his Lord: 'O Lord! All the strength I possessed I spent in inviting them, but I failed. Now my strength has given way; therefore, I supplicate to You to deal with these rebellious ones!' 'Intisar' (defend) means to defend and take retribution—meaning: 'defend me and take retribution for their rebellion.' The details of this supplication of Hazrat Nuh (peace be upon him) will follow in Surah Nuh.

"Then We opened the gates of the heaven with rain pouring down And caused the earth to burst with springs, so the waters met for a matter already predestined." (11-12)

This is a depiction of that cyclonic storm which came upon Hazrat Nuh's (peace be upon him) nation in accordance with his supplication. Its details have passed in the commentary of Surah Adh-Dhariyat. It states: We opened all the gates of the sky for them with a pouring rain, and made the earth burst with springs. Obviously, such a storm wind for which all the gates of the sky were opened would turn the earth into rivers and streams in a short time. 'so the waters met for a matter already predestined'—meaning the waters of the heavens and the earth met at that point which had been appointed by Allah Almighty, and enveloped the people of Nuh in such a way that no escape remained for anyone.

"And We carried him on a [vessel] of planks and nails," (13)

Allah's Favor is the True Protector

'of planks and nails' (dhati alwahin wa-dusur) refers, obviously, to the ship constructed by Hazrat Nuh (peace be upon him) under the specific guidance of Allah Almighty. Its mention with the details of its components is, in my view, to show the power, wisdom, favor, mercy, and majesty of Allah Almighty. The meaning is: the storm which destroyed the entire nation, and from which no one could survive, from that storm Allah Almighty saved His close servants on a vessel of planks and nails. That is: the primary thing is the favor and mercy of Allah; if it is with a person, planks and nails of wood save from the storm of Nuh, and if it is absent, massive ships sink in the blink of an eye, and robust dams are washed away like straw.

"Sailing under Our eyes as reward for he who had been denied." (14)

That is, since this ship was under Our protection and Our supervision, it continued to sail without any fear, and the waves of the storm could not bring any harm to it. It should be kept in mind: other ships must have also been present with others, but their ships were of no use to them, because they did not enjoy His protection. 'as reward for he who had been denied'—meaning Allah Almighty showed this majesty of His to support His servant who was denied, to the extent that he was compelled to supplicate: 'Indeed, I am defeated, so defend [me].'

"And We left it as a sign, so is there any who will remember?" (15)

Signs Are Numerous, but Those Who Take Warning Are Few

The referent of the object pronoun can be that land of which mention has passed with 'And caused the earth to burst with springs' (12), and also this story of Hazrat Nuh's (peace be upon him) nation, which is recorded in the Torah and has achieved the status of a general tradition in every era. In Arabic, this usage of pronouns is well-known. Several examples have passed and will come. Some have interpreted this as the structure of Nuh's ship, and stated that some predecessors had seen it on some part of Mount Judi. But this tradition holds no value with us. We will shed further light on it in the commentary of Surah Al-Haqqah. 'so is there any who will remember?' (fahal min muddakir)—meaning signs and warning events are numerous on the earth and also in history, but where are those hearts that take warning!

"And how was My punishment and My warning? And We have certainly made the Qur'an easy for remembrance, so is there any who will remember?" (16-17)

'Nudhur' is originally 'nudhuree' (My warning); the final 'ya' is dropped to observe the rhyme. It is a verbal noun from 'indhar' (warning), and means warning, reminder, and threat. The meaning is: if anyone possesses ears to hear and a heart to take warning, he can see in this story how severe is the punishment of Allah and how His threat is fulfilled.

The Meaning of 'Tayseer' (Making Easy) and 'Dhikr' (Remembrance)

This verse will occur after every story as a repeat. Understand its meaning well so that there remains no need for explanation everywhere.

Most people have interpreted this verse as: Allah Almighty has made the Qur'an easy to memorize or to receive warning from. Although this is correct in itself, the meaning is much wider. 'Tayseer' in Arabic means to equip something with accessories, make it suitable for its objective, and adorn it with all details. For example: 'yassara-l-farsa lir-rukoob' means: he trained the horse and equipped it with saddle, rein, and stirrup to make it ready for riding. Similarly, it is used for preparing a person for a mission and arming him with accessories to make him capable of it. An ignorant poet says:

'And we help our manager when some mission is faced, until we clear the path of his leadership for him.'

'Dhikr' is also used in a wide sense here. That is: teaching, reminding, warning, advice, and the establishment of proof are all included in its meaning. In all these meanings, this word has occurred in the Qur'an. It should be kept in mind: the Qur'an does not force anything upon us, but highlights those very treasures of knowledge and gnosis which Allah Almighty has imbued within our nature, and invites us to benefit from them. Having understood the correct meaning of these two words, now reflect on the context and meaning of the verse: the warning of the punishment which the Prophet is giving to you is true; the horizons, ourselves, and history are its witnesses; but you are persistent that you will believe only after seeing the punishment, whereas Allah Almighty has sent down the Qur'an for your warning and teaching, which is equipped with all accessories for this purpose; so why do you not benefit from this great blessing, why are you anxious for the whip of punishment!

Some Aspects of the Qur'an's 'Tayseer'

We have discussed 'tayseer' (making easy) in detail in our book *Mabadi'-i-Tadabbur-i-Qur'an*. There is no room to repeat them; but we will point to some necessary matters to remove the misconception of those who derive the conclusion that the Qur'an is a simple and plain Book sent down only for memorizing and reciting, standing in no need of reflection and contemplation. In their assumption, everyone who understands basic Arabic can easily understand the Qur'an. This misconception blocks the path of understanding; therefore, we must summarize those aspects of 'tayseer' to which Allah Himself has pointed:

* The most prominent aspect of 'tayseer' is that it has been sent down in 'clear Arabic' (Arabic mubeen)—meaning the eloquent standard language of the Quraysh, in which there is no complexity, so that they understand it and the proof is established against them.

* Second, He sent it down gradually, step by step, so that the Prophet (pbuh) could teach it gradually, and people could listen and understand it gradually. 'And [it is] a Qur'an which We have divided [into parts] that you might recite it to the people at intervals. And We have sent it down progressively.' (Al-Isra': 106). If the entire Qur'an had been sent down at once, as the disbelievers demanded, this would have been contrary to 'tayseer'.

* Third, all basic teachings of the Qur'an were first sent down in the form of brief, cohesive, and precise Surahs, so that they were easily received, preserved, practiced, and delivered to others. Later, when people became familiar, He explained those brief precise verses. 'A Book whose verses are perfected and then explained...' (Hud: 1) points to this very 'tayseer'.

* Fourth, every matter in the Qur'an has been described from various aspects in different forms, with different contexts and new angles, so that if a person has not understood it in one place, he understands it in another, and if one proof has not appealed to him, another does; the Qur'an has described this as 'tasreef-ul-ayat'. 'Thus do We diversify the signs...' (Al-A'raf: 58).

* Fifth, there are seven groups consisting of Meccan and Medinan Surahs in the Qur'an, which combine to form the Qur'an. In every group, themes are shared and also distinct; every Surah has its mate, and both combine to present their theme in a complete form. Under 'a consistent Book [in] pairs' (Az-Zumar: 23) and 'And We have certainly given you seven of the pairs and the great Qur'an' (Al-Hijr: 87), we have explained these اجمالات. Obviously, He chose this arrangement specifically for 'tayseer'.

5. Subsequent Theme: Verses 18–40

With the same objective for which the story of Hazrat Nuh (peace be upon him) and his nation was described above, the stories of 'Aad, Thamud, the people of Lut, and the people of Pharaoh follow. Recite the verses.

6. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

"'Aad denied. And how was My punishment and My warning?" (18)

The Story of 'Aad

After ' 'Aad denied' (Kadh-dhabat 'Aad), 'the warning' (bin-nudhur) is omitted due to contextual clarity. This is explained below, where He stated: 'Thamud denied the warning.' 'Nudhur' (warning) is the plural of 'nadhir' (warner) and also a verbal noun from 'indhar' (warning), and means warning, reminder, and threat. The meaning of the translation is determined by the context.

"Indeed, We sent upon them a screaming wind on a day of constant misfortune," (19)

This explains the punishment Allah Almighty sent upon 'Aad. It states: We sent upon them a violent wind. 'Sarsar' refers to that violent wind which blows from the north in winter, which became a cause of general misfortune. This was the period of famine in Arabia; because of this, they called it a day of misfortune (yawma nahsin). 'A day' (yawm) does not refer to a literal day, but to time and period, as is well-known. Thus, in another place, this is expressed with 'days of misfortune' (ayyam nahisat), and in some places, it is clarified that this wind was dominant over them for seven nights and eight days. 'Constant' (mustamirr) points to the reality: this wind did not blow like a temporary wind, but remained dominant over them as His punishment for seven or eight days.

"Plucking support [i.e., people] as if they were trunks of palm trees uprooted." (20)

The intensity of the wind was such: it uprooted people as if they were hollow trunks of palm trees. In Surah Al-Haqqah, in place of 'uprooted' (munqa'ir), the word 'hollow' (khawiyah) has occurred. Other places in the Qur'an indicate that when the wind began to blow, whoever was wherever remained stuck to the ground, but the wind's force increased; because of this, no one could stand up. In this manner, they all perished, and their bodies rolled in the wind like hollow trunks of palm trees. Their punishment has been described in detail in Surah Adh-Dhariyat.

"So how was My punishment and My warning? And We have certainly made the Qur'an easy for remembrance, so is there any who will remember?" (21-22)

These verses have passed at the end of the story of Nuh, and their explanation has occurred.

"Thamud denied the warning And said, 'Is it a human, one of us, whom we should follow? Indeed, we would then be in error and madness [i.e., Hell].'" (23-24)

That is, the nation of Thamud similarly denied the warning of the messenger. They said: if we follow a human like ourselves, it means we have fallen into error and Hell ('su'ur'). Generally, translators have translated 'su'ur' as madness, but here, the style in which this word has occurred is contrary to this meaning; in my view, this is the plural of 'sa'eer' and means Hell. Below in verse 47 of this Surah, it is: 'Indeed, the criminals are in error and madness [i.e., Hell]'. Obviously, this was warned by Hazrat Salih (peace be upon him) of his nation. They turned his statement against him—claiming: 'He says: if we do not accept his word, we are in error and Hell; whereas, if we follow him, we will fall into error and Hell.' Falling into Hell and going to Hell are well-known idioms. The relation between error and Hell is obvious; but no connection can be understood between error and madness.

"'Has the message been sent down upon him out of [all of] us? Rather, he is an insolent liar.'" (25)

That is: first, the very thing is strange that Allah Almighty makes a human a messenger; but what is stranger is: for this great task, He chose him from among our entire nation. Why did He ignore those who have enjoyed leadership and ancestral respect? 'Rather, he is an insolent liar'—meaning: it is impossible that this person is truthful in his claim; but he is entirely a liar and a boaster; because of this, he wants to establish his superiority over us by claiming to be a messenger of God.

"[Allah said], 'They will know tomorrow who is the insolent liar.'" (26)

This is Allah Almighty's reply on the spot—showing: whatever slanders they wish, let them. The Hour of decision is near, and His punishment is about to come, at which time they will see who is the liar and the boaster.

"Indeed, We are sending the she-camel as a trial for them, so watch them and be patient." (27)

This is the introduction to the punishment pointed to above. It states: We are sending a specific she-camel as a trial for them, so watch them and observe with patience. The meaning is: see what they do with the she-camel, and what end they face. How she was a trial is described in the subsequent verse.

"And inform them that the water is shared between them, each [one's] turn [to drink] attended." (28)

Allah Almighty specified a she-camel and commanded Hazrat Salih (peace be upon him) to inform them: 'The turn of water has been divided, and everyone must be present on their turn.' No one was allowed to interfere with her turn. If any harm was brought to her, it would be the precursor of God's punishment. This trial was appointed so that their inner corruption stood manifest, and it became clear how far their rebellion had reached. Obviously, after the warning that this she-camel was a sign of God's punishment, only those could dare to harm her who had no fear of laying their hands on the Prophet. This is that very limit of a nation's rebellion, after reaching which they deserve His punishment. After this, no other thing remains for the establishment of proof.

"But they called their companion, and he took [a sword] and hamstrung [her]." (29)

The people of the nation were not ready to tolerate this restriction. They assumed this was a mere threat of Hazrat Salih. Consequently, they appealed to their leader, who stepped forward and cut her houghs. 'Ta'ata' means to stand on one's toes to take an action—meaning in his assumption, he showed great courage and took a bold step, but this boldness destroyed the entire nation.

'Uqr' (hamstrung) means cutting the houghs. After cutting them, the camel cannot survive.

"So how was My punishment and My warning?" (30)

This has passed above. Following this event, His punishment came upon them. From another place, it is learned that they received respite only of three days to repent, but they did not benefit.

"Indeed, We sent upon them one shriek, and they became like the dry twig-rubble of a sheepfold." (31)

This points to the punishment that came upon them. 'Sayhah' means a shriek. In another place, 'sayhiqa' has also occurred. The mentor-scholar has discussed this in the commentary of Surah Adh-Dhariyat. He concluded: 'This shows that Allah Almighty sent upon them سرما's clouds, violent wind, and a terrifying shriek. But the primary destruction was due to the wind.'

It should be kept in mind: the attachments of such gurg-chamak include hail, which can become a storm of stones to turn everything into chaff. 'like the dry twig-rubble of a sheepfold' (ka-hasheem-il-muhtazir)—'muhtazir' is the one who builds a hedge. Shepherds build a hedge of dry wood and thorns in the fields to protect their flocks in the night. This hedge, after a time, is broken into pieces by rain, wind, and animals. 'Hasheem' refers to such broken pieces. This is the comparison of their destruction: the wind, rain, stones, and shriek turned them into broken pieces, as a shepherd's hedge is broken into pieces; meaning whoever looked at their houses would think that people lived here once, but now only their tramped traces remain.

"And We have certainly made the Qur'an easy for remembrance, so is there any who will remember?" (32)

This is that very same repetition which has passed above.

"The people of Lot denied the warning. Indeed, We sent upon them a storm of stones, except the family of Lot - We saved them by dawn," (33-34)

The Story of Lut's Nation

It states: the people of Lut similarly denied the warning; so after the establishment of proof, We sent upon them a storm of stones. 'Hasib' has been explained in Surah Adh-Dhariyat. 'except the family of Lot' (Illa ala Loot)—'al' is not limited to descendants, but is comprehensive of followers, as has passed. 'We saved them by dawn' (najjaynahum bi-sahar)—meaning We saved Lut (peace be upon him) and his followers by taking them out of the city before the manifestation of the punishment. Below, in 'Morning overtook them' (38), it is clarified that the punishment came in the morning, and these people had left before dawn; obviously, this protection was due to His favor.

"[As] favor from Us. Thus do We reward he who is grateful." (35)

Being saved from such a terrifying punishment is possible only of the favor and mercy of Allah. It is not possible for anyone else to make such a plan. 'Thus do We reward he who is grateful' (Kadhalika najzee man shakar) is praise for Hazrat Lut's (peace be upon him) gratitude, and also a general declaration: His favor is not specific to Hazrat Lut (peace be upon him) alone; rather, today also, those who are grateful to Him will deserve His favor. His mercy is vast.

"And he had already warned them of Our assault, but they disputed the warning." (36)

This describes why they deserved the punishment. It states: the messenger fully warned them of Our grasp, but they continuously disputed.

"And they had demanded of him his guests, so We obliterated their eyes, [saying], 'Taste My punishment and My warning.'" (37)

This points to their final audacity, after which His punishment came. It states: instead of desisting from immorality at His messenger's warning, they grew bolder, to the extent that when the angels appeared in the form of beautiful men, they came to his house and demanded that he hand over his guests to them. Those who become blind in this manner are blinded under the Sunnah of Allah, so that they are no longer capable of hearing any advice or seeing any sign; after this, the only thing remaining for them is His punishment. 'so We obliterated their eyes' (fatamasna a'yunahum) does not require that their physical eyes were closed, but it is an expression for being completely deprived of their insight. In the Qur'an, this is expressed as: 'Those are the ones whom Allah has cursed, so He deafened them and blinded their vision.' (Muhammad: 23). In the light of this verse, it means: when they reached this limit in following the desires of the self, Allah Almighty cursed them, because of which they were completely deprived of their insight. The actual beauty of eyes is in their insight, not in those physical sockets. The one who lost this is cursed by Allah, and is the target of His wrath. Moreover, when these wicked ones demanded the guests, the guests informed Hazrat Lut (peace be upon him): 'Do not worry, we are messengers of God, and they cannot reach us.' Following this, they instructed Hazrat Lut (peace be upon him) to depart. After his departure, the plausible view is that they appeared in their actual angelic form, which physical eyes could not bear to look at.

"And certainly there overtook them in the morning a constant punishment." (38)

In the morning, the constant punishment of a storm of stones came upon them. In Arabia, morning was the primary time of launched raids; because of this, the word 'morning' (bukrah) is used in this sense. It has been pointed out that Hazrat Lut (peace be upon him) had left before dawn; here, it is clarified that the punishment came after morning. This shows that his departure was completed before the punishment arrived. 'A constant punishment' (adhabun mustaqirr) refers to a punishment that stays. One kind of punishment is that which comes to warn, in which both believers and disbelievers share. The believers take warning, and the disbelievers increase in their hardness. The second is that which comes to decide the affairs of the deniers. Whichever nation faced this, was destroyed. The believers were saved, but the deniers perished. 'Mustaqirr' points to the reality: this was no temporary wind, but stayed there.

"So taste My punishment and My warning. And We have certainly made the Qur'an easy for remembrance, so is there any who will remember?" (39-40)

This is that very same repetition which has passed above, and whose explanation has occurred.

"And there certainly came to the people of Pharaoh warners. They denied Our signs - all of them - so We seized them with a seizure of one Exalted in Might and Perfect in Power." (41-42)

The Story of Pharaoh

Finally, He pointed briefly to the end of Pharaoh and his people. 'The people of Pharaoh' (Aal-e-Fir'awn) refers not only to his family, but to his entire nation. 'Nudhur' (warners) is used in the plural, and is explained by 'They denied Our signs - all of them'—meaning this points to all those signs which were manifested at the hands of Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) to warn the Egyptians, whose details are present in the Torah and the Qur'an. Every sign was sufficient to open their eyes, but their hearts were so hard that they remained blind. 'a seizure of one Exalted in Might and Perfect in Power' (akhdha 'azeezin muqtadir) points to the reality: when We seized them, We seized them as a Powerful, Competent Master seizes, from Whose grasp no one can save.

7. Subsequent Theme: Verses 43–55

Next, the concluding verses of the Surah address the leaders of the Quraysh directly to warn them: 'Look at your fate in the mirror of the history of the previous deniers. The deniers of previous messengers were destroyed; so what special distinction do you possess that you will not face the same end? If you adopt their way, you will also be disgraced in this world, and what is to come in the Hereafter will be even more severe.' Finally, the reward of the God-fearing is mentioned. Recite the verses.

8. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

Allah's Law is Identical for All

'Are your disbelievers better than those [former] ones, or have you an exemption in the scriptures?' (43) This addresses the Quraysh, who are being discussed—asking: 'You have heard the details of the end of the deniers, so when you have adopted their way, why would Allah Almighty deal with you differently? Are the disbelievers of your nation better than those, or have you been given an exemption in the heavenly scriptures—stating that do whatever you please, you will not be questioned!' The justice of Allah requires that He deals with everyone under the same law, and this is what He wrote in His scriptures; so from where did you receive this certificate of exemption! This same theme is expressed in another place as: 'Or do you have a book in which you learn That indeed for you is whatever you choose?' (Al-Qalam: 37-38).

"Or do they say, 'We are a group supporting each other'? [But] the group will be defeated, and they will turn [their] backs." (44-45)

That is, if their pride is that they are a strong group and no one can shake them, this is entirely false. No one can stand against the punishment of God. Those who are proud of their numbers and resources do not care about His warnings, but assume that they are secure; they do not understand how and from where His punishment can come! It states: if their pride is that they have managed all defenses and possess the power of defense, they should remember that their group will be of no use against His armies; they will face defeat and turn their backs.

"But the Hour is their appointment, and the Hour is more disastrous and more bitter." (46)

The Punishment of the Hereafter after the Punishment of the World

That is, they will face this defeat in this world soon, but the primary time for the manifestation of the warning is the Day of Judgment, which will be highly difficult and highly bitter for them. It should be kept in mind: the preceding verse mentioned that defeat and disgrace which the deniers of the messengers necessarily face in this world. This verse refers to that punishment which they will face in the Hereafter, and which will be highly difficult. We have referred in various places to this Sunnah of Allah—showing: the deniers necessarily face defeat in this world, and the Hereafter is the place of their disgrace. This is pointed to. 'Maw'id' (appointment) is used in the sense of time—meaning the primary time of the manifestation of the warning. 'Adha' (more disastrous) means it will be such a calamity that no one will understand anything, and all will be paralyzed.

"Indeed, the criminals are in error and madness [i.e., Hell]." (47)

The Meaning of 'Su'ur'

That is, today they say with pride: 'If we follow a human like ourselves, we will fall into error and Hell,' but on that Day they will see clearly: those who denied the messenger's warnings were in error, and the result of this error came before them in the form of Hell. 'Su'ur' is, in our view, in the sense of Hell. Its plural form points to its various depths. 'Jannat' (gardens) has also occurred in the plural in the Qur'an.

"The Day they are dragged into the Fire on their faces, [it will be said], 'Taste the touch of Hell.'" (48)

The Punishment of Arrogance

This is a depiction of that disgrace which they will face on that Day. It states: today they are showing great arrogance and denying, but that Day is coming when they will be dragged into the Fire on their faces. Being dragged on their faces is the depiction of extreme disgrace. This punishment will be meted out to them because they denied a clear reality purely out of pride. 'Taste the touch of Hell' (Dhooqoo massa saqar) can be through the language of state or of those who punish; I prefer the second. Their being dragged in this manner will tell them why they are facing this.

"Indeed, all things We created with predestination." (49)

Every Work has an Appointed Time with Allah Almighty

That is, the coming of this punishment and the Resurrection is certain. As for why they are delayed, the answer is: Allah Almighty has created everything with a specified measure and predestination. He has appointed a specified term for everything, during which He grants it respite so that it reaches its end. His dealing with nations is on this principle. If any nation adopts the path of rebellion, He does not seize it immediately, but grants it respite so that its capabilities of good and evil are manifest, so that His proof is fully established and they are left with no excuse. He will deal with you (the Quraysh) on this principle. He will grant you respite in accordance with His law so that if you wish, you may reform yourselves. And if you do not, you will fully fill your cup so that when you are seized, you have no complaint. For this reason, benefit from the respite He has granted, and do not hasten for the punishment. Its time is appointed; when it comes, no one can avert it.

"And Our command is but one, like a glance of the eye." (50)

A Single Command of Allah is Sufficient

That is: do not remain in the misconception that to bring the punishment and the Resurrection We have to make some preparations, because of which there is delay. No such obstacle is before Us. When We wish to bring it, Our single command is sufficient. There will be no need even to repeat this command, nor will We stand in need of any arrangements; but it will stand manifest as in the blink of an eye.

"And We have already destroyed your kinds, so is there any who will remember?" (51)

The Wise takes Warning from the Fate of Others

That is: why did you fall into the misconception due to the delay in the punishment that it is a mere threat? If it has not come upon you yet, it has come upon your predecessors whom We destroyed; so why do you not take warning from their states? 'Your kinds' (ashya'akum) refers to those nations whose stories have been narrated. The meaning is: the requirement of being wise is that a person takes warning from the end of others, instead of believing after everything has passed over him; what benefit will he achieve by believing then.

"And everything they did is in the written records. And every small and great [matter] is inscribed." (52-53)

Everyone's Record is Protected with Allah

That is, do not remain in the misconception that any word or deed of theirs is outside His sphere of knowledge. Whatever they have done is recorded in His registers, and every small and great matter is inscribed. Allah Almighty will place everyone's record before them, and the criminals will cry out upon seeing it: 'Wretched is this register, it has left no small or great matter without recording it.'

"Indeed, the righteous will be among gardens and rivers," (54)

The Fate of the Pious

After the criminals, the pious are mentioned—stating that they will enjoy gardens and rivers. 'The righteous' (Al-Muttaqoon) here is used in contrast to the aforementioned criminals (the deniers of reward and punishment); therefore, it refers to those who feared their Lord. The plural of 'jannat' (gardens) points to the vastness, and also indicates that everyone will receive several gardens, and within them will be various meadows. In the subsequent Surah, this will be clarified. Before 'rivers' (nahar), a prefixed noun is omitted according to the rules of Arabic—meaning: 'they will be enjoying gardens and rivers'. Gardens and rivers are mutually dependent. A garden cannot be conceived without a river. For this reason, the mention of rivers along with gardens has occurred almost everywhere in the Qur'an.

"In a seat of honor near a Sovereign, Perfect in Ability." (55) This describes the primary honor of the pious—stating that this eternal life will be granted to them in a seat of permanent honor, near the Sovereign Who is Perfect in Ability. This proximity of Allah is what makes Paradise, Paradise; otherwise, it is merely a garden. The prefixing of 'Maq'ad' (seat) to 'sidq' (honor/truth) points to its dignity, permanence, and eternity, just as is in 'lisan-e-sidq'. The meaning is: the honor of being present in His great court will neither be artificial nor temporary—such as being taken away tomorrow. 'near a Sovereign, Perfect in Ability' ('inda maleekin muqtadir)—'Maleek' contains greater force than 'Malik' (King). Conjoining it with 'muqtadir' (Perfect in Ability) is to make it even more powerful—meaning the King of the heavens and the earth is not a king in name only, as the polytheists and those deviated sects have assumed who declare God to be high above everything to make Him a marginalized entity, but He is dominant over everything. The word 'inda' (near/with) also reveals another reality: even the highest status a person achieves is to achieve a place of honor near the true Sovereign; it is not that he merges into God to become God himself, as the esoteric Sufis have claimed. By the favor of the Generous Lord, the commentary of this Surah is completed. Praise be to Allah for this. Rahman Abad. 11 August, 1977 AD. 25 Shaban, 1397 AH.
